focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By the time the B44 finally wheezed to the curb on Church Avenue, Marisol had gone from cold to damp and back to cold again. Slush soaked the edge of her jeans where it licked over her boots, and the paper bag with the clementines had started to sag at one corner. She shifted it to her other hand, felt the hard coin shape of the blue chip in her coat pocket, and looked up at Carina's building like she had to relearn where the front door was.

Carina had texted at seven in the morning.

If you're coming, come by ten. Leo has math and catechism worksheet. Don't be late.

There was no emoji and no sister-softener, just logistics, which for Carina counted as mercy.

She had mapped the morning by the hour before she slept and still landed two minutes late.

A man in a Mets cap let her into the vestibule and held the second door with his shoulder while balancing two laundry baskets. The hallway smelled like bleach over old cooking oil. One floor up it shifted to coffee, candle wax, sofrito sunk deep into plaster after fifteen years of dinners. Marisol paused outside 2B with her fist lifted, not because she forgot how to knock, but because her body remembered the last time she stood here at the level of muscle before her head caught up. She remembered the table with documents spread out, Carina's hand clamped on the suitcase handle. Leo stood in the doorway in his dinosaur pajamas, crying without sound.

She knocked anyway.

Carina opened fast, as if she had been waiting behind the door and did not want to look like she had been waiting. She had her hair in a tight ponytail and a pencil shoved through the elastic, and a stack of school forms hugged to her chest under one arm, already striped in pink highlighter. The chipped edge of her front tooth flashed when sh...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...er coats shared earbuds and laughed at something on a phone. A woman in scrubs counted singles from her wallet twice, then a third time, lips moving with each number.

At the far end of the shelter, a man in a Knicks beanie unscrewed a half-pint from a brown bag. The cap clicked. Sweet rum smell cut through diesel and fryer grease.

Marisol’s mouth flooded. Her hand found her debit card before she had decided anything, thumb rubbing the raised numbers while her body pictured the first burn down her throat and the warm drop after it.

She dug the blue day-one chip from her coat pocket and pressed the ridged edge into her thumb until it hurt. Then she hit Luis’s number.

He answered on the second ring. “Talk.”

“There’s a bottle right here.”

“Stay with me. Put your cards somewhere you can't reach fast.”

She slid debit and credit cards into the zip pocket under the document sleeves, yanked the zipper shut, and sat on the suitcase so she would have to stand, unzip, unpack, and explain herself to move.

Marisol took the folder out and checked the pages again by streetlight, as if one might have changed while she was upstairs.

Witness: Salvador Rivas.

Cause: acute combined fentanyl and heroin toxicity.

Room 214.

The bus app said the next B41 southbound would arrive in twelve minutes, then updated to fourteen, then eleven, algorithmic mercy. Marisol opened Notes and typed three words: `No stable housing.` She stared at them until they blurred and added a fourth: `Tonight.`

Luis stayed on the line while she counted headlights and kept both feet planted on the gritty sidewalk.

When the bus finally came, she told him, “Boarding,” stood, lifted the crooked suitcase, and climbed on, heading south with Mr. Han’s deadline ticking and no clean plan for where she would get off.
